metadata.title: Why swapping in the "fast" model made the whole pipeline slower
metadata.topic: Extraction-quality feedback loops in multi-document pipelines
metadata.purpose: What was never extracted cannot be rescued — paraphrase recall is a capability gap no downstream pass closes. Each rescue pass costs multiples of the original extraction, so the model that saves ~30% per document but misses 26% of facts becomes the slowest sweep end to end.
metadata.clock: narration (Kokoro af_kore) — master clock; conform each beat to measured audio
metadata.prerequisites: multi-stage LLM pipelines, recall vs. precision, cost-quality tradeoffs
metadata.exclusions: amendment-chain architecture, OCR pipeline details, contract law
metadata.visual_object: A 4-bar chart (haiku / sonnet / fable / opus), each bar three stacked segments — primary extraction, rescue, idle — the rescue segment swelling until haiku's total overtops opus's.
metadata.hook: You swap opus for haiku on a 500-contract sweep to cut costs. The sweep takes 37% longer.

beats.narration_text: The measured 500-doc benchmark: opus finishes in 389s (0.2% rescue rate); haiku finishes in 590s (26% rescue rate). The cheap model costs more total because its misses trigger reader-agent rescue passes whose latency exceeds the per-doc inference savings.
beats.scaffold_note: from Key case — cold open on the concrete instance

beats.narration_text: Lower per-doc inference cost should make the sweep cheaper and faster; this case shows the weaker model runs the whole pipeline slower — why does lower recall invert the economics?
You swap opus for haiku on a 500-contract sweep to cut costs. The sweep takes 37% longer.

What was never extracted cannot be rescued — paraphrase recall is a capability gap no downstream pass closes. Each rescue pass costs multiples of the original extraction, so the model that saves ~30% per document but misses 26% of facts becomes the slowest sweep end to end.
beats.visual: A 4-bar chart (haiku / sonnet / fable / opus), each bar three stacked segments — primary extraction, rescue, idle — the rescue segment swelling until haiku's total overtops opus's.
beats.scaffold_note: the resolving mechanism; orbits the visual object

beats.narration_text: 100 contracts. Haiku: 100×0.5s = 50s extract; 26 misses ×15s rescue = 390s; total 440s. Opus: 100×0.8s = 80s; 0.2 misses ×15s = 3s; total 83s. The "fast" model takes 5× longer.

What was never extracted cannot be rescued — paraphrase recall is a capability gap no downstream pass closes. Each rescue pass costs multiples of the original extraction, so the model that saves ~30% per document but misses 26% of facts becomes the slowest sweep end to end.
